questions.question: What are the five sections in Michael Nygard's ADR format?
options: Title, Status, Context, Decision, Consequences
Problem, Alternatives, Solution, Risks, Timeline
Background, Requirements, Design, Implementation, Testing
Summary, Stakeholders, Options, Choice, Follow-up
questions.explanation: Nygard's ADR format consists of Title (a short noun phrase), Status (proposed/accepted/deprecated/superseded), Context (the forces at play), Decision (the specific choice made), and Consequences (positive, negative, and neutral outcomes).

questions.question: When should you write an ADR?
options: For every code change, no matter how small
Only when migrating to a new programming language
For significant architectural choices that would be expensive or painful to reverse
After the project is complete, as documentation
questions.explanation: ADRs are for significant architectural decisions — technology selections, API design choices, data model decisions, structural choices — where the decision would be expensive or painful to reverse. Writing ADRs for every code change creates noise; writing them only for language migrations is too narrow; writing them after the project loses the real-time context that makes ADRs valuable.

questions.question: What must the Consequences section of an ADR include to be well-written?
options: Only the positive outcomes of the decision
Positive, negative, and neutral outcomes with honest trade-offs
A detailed implementation timeline
Links to the relevant source code files
questions.explanation: A well-written Consequences section must include positive, negative, and neutral outcomes with honest trade-offs. Listing only positives makes the ADR a marketing document rather than a decision record. Implementation timelines belong in project plans, not ADRs. Source code links are useful but not required in the Consequences section.

questions.question: An ADR with status 'Superseded by ADR-0012' means:
options: The ADR was deleted because it was wrong
A newer ADR (ADR-0012) replaces this decision, and the old ADR remains in the log for historical reference
The ADR is still valid but should be read alongside ADR-0012
The ADR was rejected during the proposal phase
questions.explanation: Superseded means a newer ADR replaces the old decision. The original ADR is never deleted — it remains in the log as a historical record of the decision trail. ADR numbers are never reused. 'Rejected' is a different status, and deleted ADRs lose institutional memory.

questions.question: What is the key difference between an ADR and an RFC?
options: An ADR is longer than an RFC
An RFC records a decision; an ADR proposes a change
An ADR records a decision already made; an RFC proposes a change before a decision is reached
There is no difference — they are interchangeable
questions.explanation: An ADR (Architecture Decision Record) records a decision that has been made — its purpose is to preserve the reasoning. An RFC (Request for Comments) proposes a change before a decision is reached — its purpose is to gather input. They serve different purposes: ADRs are immutable records; RFCs are mutable proposals. An RFC process may result in an ADR.

questions.question: Why should rejected ADRs be kept in the ADR log?
options: Because deleting files from version control is difficult
They document paths not taken and prevent re-proposing the same idea without new information
They are required for compliance audits
They serve as templates for future ADRs
questions.explanation: Rejected ADRs document paths not taken. When someone later proposes the same approach, the rejected ADR provides context about why it was rejected. Without this record, teams may waste time re-proposing and re-evaluating the same idea. Keeping rejected ADRs is a deliberate practice, not a version control convenience.
